title: Goodman upbeat on race for AI riches
full_text:
Billionaire Greg Goodman is confident Australia will claim its share of the artificial-intelligence revolution’s gains, citing Australia’s strong competitive position as global costs surge.
The nation’s competitiveness in the emerging field has been under scrutiny amid warnings that Australia risks missing out on a once-in-a-generation AI investment boom worth up to $US1 trillion ($1.4 trillion) globally that could lift productivity and raise living standards.
The issue is coming to a head locally as technology companies and data centres race to lay the foundations for the fast-growing industry, even as they run up against power, water and planning restrictions.
Mr Goodman, who leads the listed Goodman Group, which has a multibillion-dollar pipeline of sites in Sydney and Melbourne, said the conditions were in place for Australia to succeed.
“If we can get the infrastructure done at a reasonable cost and power is at a reasonable cost, and the availability thereof, we’re competitive because other parts of the world are also getting more expensive with planning issues and grid issues,” he said.
The market is also splitting between large companies, which are able to deliver the huge projects and which attract hyperscaler and AI customers, and more speculative developers without access to power or big customers.
“We’ve seen that around the world, because the hurdles are getting higher in regard to infrastructure and costs,” Mr Goodman said. “Equity capital is becoming more selective, with investors and customers both wanting partners with financial capacity, strong alignment and a proven track record of delivery.” There has been a rush in Australia of new entrants promising to develop data centres, and some traditional property developers are looking to cash in by speculatively promoting data centres.
“You’ve got to have the capital; you’ve got to have the contracting locked down to know what you’re going to deliver,” Mr Goodman said. “Otherwise … don’t open your mouth and start running around, trying to negotiate an outcome where you do not know whether that’s going to end up.” Goodman has a $14.5bn development work book – of which data centres make up 73 per cent – and this is expected to hit about $18bn next month, with yields on the projects rising.
Mr Goodman said AI adoption was accelerating while compute capacity was constrained globally. He predicted more demand for computing power in big cities as AI models shifted from training to inferencing.
The global hunt for powered sites – where Goodman has a head start due to its existing industrial holdings – is gathering pace. “Energy availability is the most significant constraint to delivering the infrastructure needed to power the digital economy,” Mr Goodman said.
He called out the extraordinary scale of data centre investment required to meet customer demand, saying this was “likely to exceed the current funding capacity of global capital markets”.
“The large gap between demand and what can be delivered is expected to grow as a result of these supply constraints,” he said, adding that the company was seeing a wave of demand.
Goodman delivered a solid update in which the company confirmed earnings guidance of at least 9 per cent operating earnings per security growth for this financial year, although the market sold down the stock, partly as some investors expected an upgrade. Its shares fell 4c to $30.01.
Both the traditional industrial property and data centre developing arms of the business are on track, with Mr Goodman saying the company was not taking a big bet on any one market.
He said the company was now at the “customer stage” at key projects and demand was strong, although analysts, which have put the company under scrutiny as it rolls out data centres, wanted more news on leasing.
Bell Potter analyst Andy MacFarlane said Goodman had reiterated guidance, which the market was watching but not necessarily expecting. On the data centre front, he said the company’s power bank increased 7 per cent to 6.4GW, with work-in-progress increasing. Mr Goodman pointed to success the company had around the world in bringing partners into its projects and said it was on track to do so in Australia.
